questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Wild animals - strict liability for dangerous propensities
questions.stem: A private animal exhibitor kept a tiger in a reinforced enclosure that met all industry standards. During a severe storm, a tree unexpectedly fell and damaged the enclosure, allowing the tiger to escape. Before the exhibitor could recapture it, the tiger attacked a passerby on a nearby road. The passerby sued the exhibitor.
questions.call: Is the exhibitor likely liable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a keeper of a wild animal is strictly liable for harm resulting from the animal's dangerous propensities.
choices.rationale: Tigers are wild animals, and strict liability applies to harms characteristic of their dangerous nature even if the keeper used reasonable care.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the exhibitor used reasonable care in constructing the enclosure.
choices.rationale: Due care is not a defense to strict liability for wild animals when the harm flows from the animal's dangerous propensity.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the storm was unexpected.
choices.rationale: An act of nature may matter in some contexts, but the injury was caused by the escaped tiger's characteristic dangerousness.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the tiger had attacked someone before.
choices.rationale: For wild animals, strict liability does not require proof of the keeper's prior knowledge of a particular animal's viciousness.

explanation.testedIssue: Strict liability for injuries caused by a wild animal.
explanation.governingRule: A possessor of a wild animal is strictly liable for harm caused by the animal's dangerous propensities characteristic of its class.
explanation.application: A tiger's attack is exactly the type of harm that makes tigers dangerous wild animals. Reasonable enclosure precautions do not eliminate strict liability.
explanation.commonTrap: Using negligence concepts such as reasonable care for a wild-animal strict-liability claim.
explanation.memoryAid: Wild animal, characteristic harm, strict liability.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Fourth Amendment - scope of search under warrant
questions.stem: Police obtained a valid warrant to search a suspect's apartment for a stolen mountain bicycle and bicycle repair tools. While executing the warrant, an officer opened a small locked jewelry box on a dresser. The box was far too small to contain a bicycle or any repair tool described in the warrant. Inside the box, the officer found illegal pills. The suspect moved to suppress the pills.
questions.call: Should the court grant the motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a valid warrant to search an apartment permits opening every container inside.
choices.rationale: The permissible scope of a warrant search is limited to places and containers where the described items could reasonably be found.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the jewelry box could not reasonably contain any item listed in the warrant.
choices.rationale: Officers may search only areas where the objects of the warrant might be located. The box was too small for the specified items.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the pills were contraband and therefore automatically admissible.
choices.rationale: Contraband found during an unlawful search is subject to suppression unless an exception applies.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only because the jewelry box was locked.
choices.rationale: The decisive issue is not the lock but the fact that the box could not contain the items named in the warrant.

explanation.testedIssue: The permissible scope of a search conducted under a warrant.
explanation.governingRule: A warrant authorizes police to search the places and containers where the items described in the warrant could reasonably be found.
explanation.application: A small jewelry box could not hold a mountain bicycle or repair tools described in the warrant. Opening it exceeded the warrant's scope.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating a premises warrant as permission to inspect every closed container regardless of the objects sought.
explanation.memoryAid: The object of the search defines where police may look.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
questions.subtopic: Opposing-party statement - employee statement within scope
questions.stem: In a negligence action against a delivery company, the plaintiff called a customer-service supervisor employed by the company. The supervisor had investigated the incident as part of her job the day after it occurred and emailed the plaintiff, “Our driver admits he entered the alley too fast during yesterday's delivery.” The plaintiff offers the email against the delivery company to prove the driver's speed. The company objects on hearsay grounds.
questions.call: Is the email admissible against the company?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because only statements by the company's president can be offered against the company.
choices.rationale: Statements by an employee or agent may qualify if made on a matter within the scope of the relationship while it existed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the supervisor did not personally witness the delivery.
choices.rationale: The statement is offered as an opposing-party statement. Personal knowledge may affect weight or create additional hearsay issues depending on the embedded source, but the supervisor's statement within her job scope is not excluded on this basis alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because it was made by the company's employee on a matter within the scope of her employment while that relationship existed.
choices.rationale: An employee's statement on a matter within the scope of employment, made during the relationship, is treated as an opposing-party statement when offered against the employer.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the supervisor is unavailable to testify at trial.
choices.rationale: Opposing-party statements do not require declarant unavailability.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an employee's statement may be admitted against an employer as an opposing-party statement.
explanation.governingRule: A statement offered against an opposing party and made by the party's agent or employee on a matter within the scope of that relationship and while it existed is not hearsay under the Federal Rules.
explanation.application: The supervisor was employed by the company and investigated delivery incidents as part of her job when she sent the email. The statement concerned that job-related investigation.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming only formal admissions by top officers can bind an organization evidentially.
explanation.memoryAid: Employee plus scope plus during employment equals opposing-party statement.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: UCC firm offer by merchant
questions.stem: A wholesale lumber merchant signed and sent a letter to a builder stating, “We offer to sell you 10,000 board feet of oak at $4 per board foot. This offer will remain open for 60 days.” The builder gave no consideration for the promise to keep the offer open. Thirty days later, the merchant attempted to revoke. The builder received the revocation and immediately accepted the original offer in writing.
questions.call: Was the merchant's attempted revocation effective?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because an offer is always revocable until accepted unless the offeree paid consideration.
choices.rationale: Under the UCC, a merchant's signed firm offer can be irrevocable without consideration for the stated time, up to three months.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the transaction involved a large quantity of lumber.
choices.rationale: The quantity does not make the firm offer revocable. The merchant's signed assurance controls.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, but only because the builder accepted before receiving the revocation.
choices.rationale: The facts state the builder received the revocation before accepting. The revocation was ineffective because of the firm-offer rule.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a signed written assurance by a merchant to hold open an offer for goods is irrevocable for the stated 60-day period despite lack of consideration.
choices.rationale: The UCC firm-offer rule makes the merchant's signed offer irrevocable for the stated period, not exceeding three months.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a merchant's signed written promise to hold open an offer for goods is irrevocable without consideration.
explanation.governingRule: Under UCC Article 2, an offer by a merchant to buy or sell goods in a signed writing that gives assurance it will be held open is not revocable for the stated time, or a reasonable time if none is stated, not exceeding three months.
explanation.application: The lumber merchant signed a written offer for goods and promised to keep it open for 60 days. The attempted revocation on day 30 was ineffective.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying the common-law option rule and requiring consideration despite the UCC firm-offer rule.
explanation.memoryAid: Merchant plus signed goods offer plus hold-open assurance equals firm offer up to three months.
